rRO3gqGX8IQ.title: Learn Nyanja 101 - Basic Phrases
rRO3gqGX8IQ.prompt_used: This is a Nyanja (Chinyanja) language lesson for English speakers. The instructor teaches practical Nyanja for everyday situations. Common Nyanja: moni (hello), muli bwanji (how are you), ndili bwino ...

segments.text: Hey everybody, it's the Zambian-American, or muli bwanji, indine Zambian-American.
So, I'm back again. It's been a long while, but here's what I got.
I would love to really revamp this channel and actually start putting out more content.
So, that is my goal.
And so, what we're going to be doing is we're going to be walking through the Town Nyanja book that I've shared.
And I'll put the link for that. You can buy that on Amazon, just less than $10. It's awesome.
But, here's the thing. When you read the book, you can't actually hear the sounds.
So, my goal is really to show you what the sounds sound like.
Like, how do you say each specific word?
So, if you have the Town Nyanja book, open it up to page 31.
In that, Useful Phrases.
So, in this book, everybody asks when they go to Zambia, how do you say hi?
So, for hi, it's just bwanji.
And it really depends on how you say it.
If you just said bwanji, that's just like, hey.
But if you said bwanji, like raising up your tone like that, you're asking a question like, hey, what's up?
How are you?
So, how are you is muli bwanji.
Muli bwanji.
Then, the response to that is niribwino or nirimuse.
Niribwino is I'm fine or I'm good.
Nirimuse, it's good or some people have even translated it to it's proper.
Now, another response to that may be nangaimwe, which is basically saying and you.
And so, that's a chance.
So, if you say niribwino nangaimwe, it's like saying I'm good and how about you?
And then your response should be wino, which is I'm fine.
All right.
Moving on.
For a lot of people that have been to Zambia or know this, this is just so easy.
But we're going to go through it for especially those people that have not.
But good morning is moka bwanji.
And that can also be translated to how did you wake?
So, if somebody asks you in English, how did you wake?
They're really asking you moka bwanji, which is good morning.
So, how did you wake?
Then, your response is naokabwino, which is I've woken well.
Naoka, you're putting the possessive like I'm waking well.
Naokabwino.
Okay.
So, if somebody says moka bwanji, you say naokabwino.
Okay.
And now, for good afternoon, it is machamabwanji.
And then, the response is nachomabwino.
Okay.
Nachomabwino.
That is the response for machamabwanji.
So, machamabwanji, good afternoon.
Nachomabwino.
It's good.
It's a good afternoon.
Okay.
So, there's your good morning and good afternoon.
Next one, good night.
You're going to say mugwanebwino, which is you sleep well.
Okay.
Mugwane, it's putting the possessive on you like you're saying it to somebody.
So, you're saying good night to somebody or also translated to sleep well.
Now, the next one, goodbye.
Okay.
When you're saying goodbye to somebody, there's two ways to say it.
Like, are you the one leaving or is your friend the one who's leaving?
So, if you want to say go well, you say mugwanebwino.
Mugwanebwino.
Okay.
Mu is talking about the you.
So, remember that you right there, that's going to put the possession on the other person.
Mugwanebwino because you're talking to somebody.
Now, if you want to say, if you are the one leaving and you want to tell your friend to stay well
or to remain well, you say mugwanebwino.
Okay.
Again, you're saying it to somebody else.
So, you have to say mugwanebwino or mugwanebwino.
If you said mugwanebwino, you're saying like I'm going well.
But you're saying it to your friend like you go well.
Makes sense?
Hopefully, it does.
The next one, see you.
Dzaonana.
Okay.
Dzaonana.
Usually, people couple this with the word for tomorrow.
So, dzaonanamairo is how they would put that together.
The next one, excuse me.
Zikomo.
Now, this often, you know, excuse me isn't commonly.
I mean, it is, it does mean, zikomo and excuse me do mean the same thing.
But it's usually kind of like you're in a crowded situation.
You just need to get by somebody.
You just say zikomo.
The word for yes is eh.
Or the word for no is yay.
So, but a lot of times, people can just say yes or no.
Physical characteristics that you'll see in a yes is raising up the eyebrows and lifting up the chin and the head.
That's a, they lift that up and kind of give you the eyebrows.
That means yes.
And also another thing that may be a little bit tricky, but if you're asking a question that is such a way that it's a negative thing, they're going to answer to the affirmative of it.
So, saying like you didn't go outside, they'll say yes.
So, did you go outside?
No.
They answered it the proper way, but a lot of times, English users, especially in America, we answer to the negative to what people want to hear.
So, it's really more fault on the question asker than the one who's responding.
I just had to make a little tangent about that real quick.
So, back to this, but the word for thank you is zikomo.
So, excuse me, thank you, all interchangeable.
And to say thank you very much, zikomo kwambiri.
And if you're looking at the word as it's written there, kwambiri, you'll be like where is the L and the R?
And I'm going to get to that, but L and R are very similar.
So, zikomo kwambiri, but I'm not doing kwambili.
If you're saying kwambili, it's wrong.
It's kwambiri, okay?
Make it just a really soft R sound.
On that, zikomo kwambiri, thank you very much.
Okay?
The next ones.
These are, I think, probably just like the foundation of a lot of, when I started learning Nyanja, this is the foundation of what I learned.
And that is nifuna, which is I want.
So, nifuna, I want.
Sinifuna, I don't want.
Okay?
Sinifuna, nifuna manzi, I want water.
Sinifuna manzi, I don't want water.
So, you can use that and then attach on any noun that you want to put on.
Or you can make that like you can tack on another verb.
So, like nifuna nayanda kunyumba, I want to go home.
Okay?
Because there were two verbs on that.
The next one is I like, which is nikonda.
Okay?
And we'll get to this a little bit later, but it depends on how you're going to use it or say it as far as how that's going to be said.
Then the inverse of that is sinikonda.
I don't like.
So, if you notice there, you have the SI in front of the word.
But when you put the SI in front of it, it is the opposite or you don't want that.
So, that's a really good foundational piece to take and store away.
Like in English, there are always exceptions for things, but nifuna and sinifuna, nikonda, sinikonda.
Okay?
So, you really, in a lot of ways, can use the SI on the front of a word to say don't or mean the opposite of that.
Next, I think.
Niganiza.
Niganiza, I think.
Ni is the I and then ganiza to think.
Okay?
So, if you kind of have that root verb and then we're going to put the prefix on there, ni for the I, then what's this?
Nichichaniichi.
This one is tough.
And I won't blame if Nyanja is going to be a second or third language for you if this is tough to say.
Nichichaniichi.
What's this?
So, I don't often use that phrase when I'm in Zambia.
Nichichaniichi.
But that is just how you would say what's this.
I don't know.
This is the next phrase.
Siniziba.
Now, if you're looking on page 31 of the Town Nyanja book, you'll see that there's a different, it's an interesting character.
It's like a B, but at the bottom of the B, it's a little tail on the end of it.
That B sound is going to be a B and V sound together.
So, Siniziba.
So, if you do Siniziba or Siniziva like a V is in violet, then you're wrong.
Okay?
It's B and V together.
So, merged.
Va.
So, imagine closing your lips a little bit and pushing out between your lips the BV sound.
Siniziva.
Okay?
Hopefully, that'll help you make that right sound.
Do you speak English?
Muziba kukamba chizungu.
Muziba is do you know kukamba to speak chizungu, which is English.
Muziba kukamba chizungu.
So, since you're saying it to somebody, you're asking them.
So, just in the previous one, it said Siniziba.
You'll see below Muziba, they're talking about you know kukamba, you speak chizungu.
You know you speak chizungu if like you're translating it in the hard way.
But that's not actually how it's understood.
Muziba kukamba chizungu.
Then, obviously, you'll be like, you know, if they're saying that and you can get that, that's great.
Very impressed.
I don't speak Nyanja.
This is great if you are learning to speak Nyanja.
You can just say this and it's hilarious because the kids give a great response to it.
So, I don't speak Nyanja.
Siniziba kukamba chinyanja.
Siniziba, I don't know how to speak Nyanja.
Siniziba kukamba chinyanja.
But you can also say Sinimakamba chinyanja.
So, you can say Siniziba kukamba chinyanja or you can say Sinimakamba chinyanja.
Those both mean, I don't speak Nyanja.
And likely when you do say that, it's really funny for where we work with kids.
I'll say, Siniziba kukamba chinyanja.
Then they'll say, Batukamba, kukamba kukamba.
Like they'll say, but you're speaking right now.
You're saying like, I don't speak Nyanja.
No habla Espanol.
But you're speaking it and saying it.
So, I speak a bit of Nyanja.
Sinimakamba pangono chinyanja.
Sinimakamba pangono chinyanja.
I speak a bit of chinyanja.
So, that would be really funny to say.
And then I'm learning to speak Nyanja which is what I always...
This is a great one to file away because if you're talking to a Nyanja speaker
and you say this, it instantly increases your street cred.
So, Nipunzila kukamba chinyanja.
I'm learning to speak Nyanja.
I'll break that down.
Nipunzila kukamba chinyanja.
I'm learning to speak Nyanja.
Okay?
And if you're like...
If somebody's like, okay, and they're going to speak Nyanja to you
and you have no idea what they're saying,
you just say, sinimvela.
Or you could say, sinimvela bueno.
Like, I don't understand or I don't understand well.
Now, what's interesting is to understand and to hear are so closely related.
So, if you say, I didn't hear.
Sininamvele.
Okay?
It's very similar.
Sinimvela.
I don't understand.
Sininamvele.
I didn't hear what you said.
Okay.
Almost there.
A few more on this one.
Please repeat that.
Bwezani pone na futi.
Bwezani pone na futi.
Please repeat that.

Kambani pangono pangono.
Which is basically, speak just beat by beat.
Just slow, slow.

Mumakamba bwanji water Muchen Nyanja.
You're saying, how do you say water in Muchen Nyanja?
And they'll say, manzi.
That's how you say it.

0fORE5Z0MmA.title: Learn Nyanja 101 - Shopping

whenever Zambia became a nation in October of 1964,
the president, the great Kenneth Kaunda,
made a statement to Zambia,
to encourage them to intermarry with all the tribes,
so that they would become one Zambia, one nation.

that people can still have their tribal identity,

because Zambians are the most friendly people in the whole entire world, hands down.

gAc6jEbXg6w.title: Learn Nyanja for Business / Work
gAc6jEbXg6w.prompt_used: This is a Nyanja (Chinyanja) language lesson for English speakers. The instructor teaches practical Nyanja for everyday situations. Common Nyanja: moni (hello), muli bwanji (how are you), ndili bwino ...

segments.text: Muli bwanji and tikulandi-landi, hello, welcome back to another episode of The Zambian American.
In this episode, we'll talk about Nyanja related to work and business.
Now, it's important to understand the difference between the word work and job.
So, to work is sebenza. Sebenza. Remember, that's a BV sound. Sebenza.
Now, job is njito or kunjito. Njito is job, sebenza to work.
So, if you want to ask somebody, where do you work? You say, mu sebenza kuti? Where do you work?
Mu is talking about the you, sebenza is work, kuti where? Where do you work? Mu sebenza kuti?
Now, if you want to say, I work here, you say, ni, which is the I, ni sebenza panu. Ni sebenza panu.
That is, I work here.
Now, if you want to say, what you do, ni gwira, ni njito, ya makaniki.
Ni gwira, ni njito, ya Bible teacher.
Okay, that was, I work as, and then you would give the title.
Ni gwira, ni njito, ya makaniki.
Okay, that's your title.
Now, if you're looking for a certain person, and you want to say, like, I'm looking for Mr. So-and-so, you say, ni huna va Mr. Melenga.
I'm looking for Mr. Melenga.
Ni huna va Mr. Melenga.
Or, ni huna va Mrs. Sichoni.
Or, va Mrs. Melenga.
Mwamwela, do you understand?
Mwamwela is, do you understand?
If you are getting me, write a comment saying, I'm getting you.
Manje, ufunika ukamba chinyanja, ufunika kamba, ni mwela, I understand.
I'm understanding you.
Write, ni mwela, I'm understanding.
Okay, vadi, next one.
The next thing that we can do is, we can talk about days.
So, when you want to meet with someone.
So, when you ask the question, when can we meet?
Tizaku manaliti, tizaku manaliti, when can we meet?
Now, the next thing they're going to say is, okay, manje, we'll meet now.
Now, now, manje, manje, is now, now.
Or, you say, today.
Lelo is today.
Lelo.
Now, if you say, maero.
Now, if you're going to meet with someone, maero also means tomorrow, and it means yesterday.
So, manje, if they say maero, you can understand they are talking about tomorrow, okay?
Not yesterday.
So, the way I like to do this is, if I'm talking to somebody and said it already happened in the past,
I point back to my left.
If I'm saying it's in the future, I like to point forward to my right.
I don't always get the context right on certain things, so I try to help explain the context.
Like, maero, tomorrow, I kind of point to the front.
